stem:
A 22-year-old man with a known diagnosis of schizophrenia is brought from his community accommodation by his case manager. Over 5 days he has progressively stopped eating, drinking, and speaking. On examination he is mute, holds postures imposed on him for over a minute (waxy flexibility), and shows echolalia when prompted. He has not eaten in 48 hours. He is on no current antipsychotic having stopped his depot 3 months ago because of side effects. He has no fever. His Bush-Francis catatonia rating scale score is 18. Bedside investigations have just returned.

The team is now planning treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 110/min, blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, waxy flexibility, echolalia, posturing; no rigidity, no clonus; mildly dry mucous membranes
data_table.Investigations: CK 320 U/L; creatinine 95 micromol/L; sodium 138 mmol/L; CRP 6 mg/L; urine drug screen negative; CT brain normal
data_table.Medications: Nil current; last paliperidone depot 3 months ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Lorazepam 2 mg intramuscularly as a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge with reassessment at 30 minutes.
options.rationale: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IM is the standard catatonia lorazepam challenge: rapid improvement supports diagnosis and confirms first-line treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Restart paliperidone palmitate depot 100 mg intramuscularly today as first-line treatment of the relapse.
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can precipitate or worsen catatonia, particularly malignant catatonia, and are not first-line here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Refer for urgent electroconvulsive therapy without trialling a benzodiazepine challenge first.
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in catatonia but is second-line after a trial of lorazepam unless features are life-threatening. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Bromocriptine 5 mg orally three times daily targeting central dopamine receptor agonism over 7 days.
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is used in neuroleptic malignant syndrome, which is excluded here by normal temperature, normal CK, and no rigidity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg orally now and reassessment at 24 hours of the catatonic features by the team.
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can precipitate or worsen catatonia and are not the diagnostic or therapeutic step at this stage. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a treatable syndrome of motor and behavioural features (mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, echolalia or echopraxia, negativism, stereotypy) recognised by Bush-Francis scoring. The lorazepam challenge with 1 to 2 mg IM is both diagnostic and therapeutic: marked improvement within 30 minutes supports the diagnosis and warrants regular lorazepam (often 4 to 16 mg daily in divided doses). Electroconvulsive therapy is second-line if lorazepam fails or for malignant catatonia (fever, autonomic instability, rhabdomyolysis). Antipsychotics may worsen catatonia and are generally avoided in the acute phase.
key_points: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IM as the standard catatonia challenge.
Antipsychotics can precipitate or worsen catatonia in the acute phase.
ECT is second-line, and first-line in malignant catatonia with autonomic instability.
explanation.pearls: Catatonia plus fever, autonomic instability, and rising CK should be treated as malignant catatonia and referred for urgent ECT rather than persisting with benzodiazepines.

stem:
A 27-year-old man is referred to your community mental health service. Over the last 3 years he has had two clearly defined manic episodes lasting 4 to 6 weeks with elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiose delusions, and pressured speech, each requiring inpatient admission. Between episodes, in periods totalling over 12 months out of the last 3 years, he has had persistent auditory hallucinations of a derogatory commentary voice and persecutory delusions about being followed by ASIO, without prominent mood symptoms during that time. He is currently euthymic on valproate 1000 mg daily but still hears the voice. Substance use, organic causes, and recent stressors have been excluded.

The team is preparing his diagnosis and treatment plan.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 76/min, blood pressure 124/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Euthymic, organised speech; reports persecutory belief about being monitored and ongoing voices
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U+E, LFTs, TFTs, B12, folate, and CRP normal; CT brain normal; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features confined to episodes of severe mania.
options.rationale: Persistent psychotic symptoms across 12 cumulative months between mood episodes excludes simple bipolar I with psychotic features. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type, because psychotic symptoms persist outside mood episodes.
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms in the absence of major mood symptoms during the lifetime of illness. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Schizophrenia with comorbid recurrent major depressive disorder rather than schizoaffective disorder.
options.rationale: Clear discrete manic episodes meeting bipolar criteria favour schizoaffective bipolar type over schizophrenia plus depression. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Delusional disorder coexisting with bipolar I disorder, because the delusions are non-bizarre persistent.
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires functioning outside the delusion to be relatively preserved and excludes prominent hallucinations. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder with recurrent affective episodes triggered by past stimulant use.
options.rationale: Urine drug screen is negative and the symptoms persist outside any substance use, excluding this diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP schizophrenia guideline)

explanation.summary: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires (a) an uninterrupted period of illness during which there is a major mood episode (depressive or manic) concurrent with Criterion A of schizophrenia; (b) at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations during the lifetime of illness in the absence of a major mood episode; (c) mood episodes present for the majority of the total duration of illness; (d) not better explained by substance use or other medical condition. Persistent psychosis during 12 cumulative euthymic months distinguishes schizoaffective disorder from bipolar I with psychotic features.
key_points: Schizoaffective bipolar type requires at least 2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms in the lifetime.
Mood symptoms must be present for the majority of total illness duration.
Bipolar I with psychotic features confines psychosis to mood episodes.
explanation.pearls: Document the timing of psychosis relative to mood episodes carefully across the lifetime course; the diagnosis hinges on whether psychotic symptoms outlast mood episodes by at least 2 weeks.

stem:
A 31-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, two prior manic episodes (one with psychotic features), and two prior depressive episodes, has been stable on sodium valproate 1500 mg daily for 4 years with no episodes. She presents to your perinatal psychiatry clinic with a positive home pregnancy test today. Her last menstrual period was 6 weeks ago. She had not been planning pregnancy. She is otherwise well, has no current mood symptoms, takes folate 500 micrograms daily, and lives with a supportive partner. She is well engaged with treatment, has private health insurance, and has no comorbid substance use. She is asking what to do about her medication.

Her most recent bloods are 2 months old.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 78/min, blood pressure 116/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Euthymic, normal mental state examination; no neurological abnormality
data_table.Investigations: Bedside beta-hCG positive; pelvic ultrasound to be arranged; FBC, U+E, LFTs, and TSH 2 months ago all normal; valproate level 480 micromol/L
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 1500 mg daily; folate 500 micrograms daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate at current dose because abrupt change risks a relapse during organogenesis.
options.rationale: Valproate carries 10% major congenital malformation and 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental disability rates and is contraindicated in pregnancy unless no alternative. (Source: RANZCP perinatal mood disorder guideline)

options.text: Stop valproate abruptly today and reintroduce only if a manic relapse occurs during the pregnancy.
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of valproate without substitution carries about 50 percent risk of relapse in the first trimester. (Source: RANZCP perinatal mood disorder guideline)

options.text: Cross-titrate valproate to quetiapine over 4 weeks, increase folate to 5 mg daily, and arrange dating scan.
options.rationale: Valproate carries 10% major malformation risk and 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental risk; substitution by a safer mood stabiliser is recommended. (Source: RANZCP perinatal mood disorder guideline)

options.text: Switch to lithium carbonate 750 mg at night during the first trimester then return to valproate later.
options.rationale: Lithium during organogenesis carries a small Ebstein anomaly risk and is generally avoided in the first trimester if a safer alternative exists. (Source: RANZCP perinatal mood disorder guideline)

options.text: Add lamotrigine 25 mg daily to valproate, intending to taper valproate after the first trimester ends.
options.rationale: Valproate strongly inhibits lamotrigine clearance, doubling Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk; combining the two during titration is unsafe. (Source: AMH antiepileptic interactions)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate has the highest teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risk of any mood stabiliser: approximately 10% major congenital malformations (notably neural tube, cardiac, and craniofacial) and 30 to 40% rate of neurodevelopmental disability including reduced IQ and autism spectrum disorder. Australian and TGA pregnancy prevention requirements mean valproate should not be used in women of childbearing potential unless no alternative works. When unplanned pregnancy occurs, cross-titrate to a safer agent (quetiapine, lamotrigine if not on valproate, or lithium balancing first trimester Ebstein risk against later trimester safety) over 2 to 4 weeks, increase folate to 5 mg daily, arrange dating ultrasound, and refer to perinatal psychiatry.
key_points: Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy unless no alternative exists.
Cross-titrate over 2 to 4 weeks to avoid relapse; quetiapine and lamotrigine are commonly substituted.
Increase folate to 5 mg daily for 12 weeks of pregnancy on any anticonvulsant.
explanation.pearls: If valproate truly cannot be substituted, the patient must be counselled about the residual risk and offered detailed morphology scanning at 18 to 20 weeks plus paediatric neurodevelopmental follow-up.

stem:
A 38-year-old man newly diagnosed with HIV attends his sexual health clinic appointment for review of staging results. He is asymptomatic with no constitutional symptoms, no respiratory symptoms, and no oral candidiasis. His CD4 count is 250 cells/microlitre and HIV viral load 86,000 copies/mL. He has no other medical conditions, no sulfa allergy, and no current medications. He is keen to start antiretroviral therapy and asks about additional prophylaxis given his low CD4 count.

The clinic registrar has called you to clarify the prophylaxis plan.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 76/min, blood pressure 120/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7°C
data_table.Examination: Well, no oral thrush; lymphadenopathy minimal in cervical chains; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: CD4 250 cells/microlitre; HIV RNA 86,000 copies/mL; Toxoplasma IgG positive; cryptococcal antigen negative; chest X-ray clear; LFTs and renal function normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prophylaxis to commence?

options.text: Azithromycin 1200 mg orally once weekly to prevent Mycobacterium avium complex infection.
options.rationale: MAC prophylaxis is recommended when CD4 falls below 50 cells/microlitre, not at 250. (Source: ASHM HIV management guideline)

options.text: Fluconazole 400 mg orally once weekly to prevent invasive cryptococcal meningitis disease.
options.rationale: Cryptococcal prophylaxis is not routinely recommended in Australia; cryptococcal antigen screening is used to drive pre-emptive therapy at lower CD4 thresholds. (Source: ASHM HIV management guideline)

options.text: Pyrimethamine 50 mg plus sulfadiazine 1 g daily for toxoplasmosis prophylaxis given positive IgG.
options.rationale: Routine toxoplasmosis prophylaxis is initiated when CD4 falls below 100 with positive IgG; cotrimoxazole covers both at 250. (Source: ASHM HIV management guideline)

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally once daily for Pneumocystis prophylaxis.
options.rationale: ASHM recommends Pneumocystis prophylaxis with cotrimoxazole when CD4 falls below 200 cells/microlitre or below 14 percent, and at 200 to 250 borderline cases. (Source: ASHM HIV management guideline)

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg daily for 6 months as latent tuberculosis prophylaxis regardless of QFT result.
options.rationale: Latent TB therapy depends on a positive QuantiFERON or tuberculin test, not on HIV status alone. (Source: ASHM HIV management guideline)

explanation.summary: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis in HIV is indicated when CD4 falls below 200 cells/microlitre, when the CD4 percentage falls below 14, or with a previous PJP episode. First-line is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (cotrimoxazole) 160/800 mg orally daily, with weekly thrice-daily dosing as an alternative. This dose also covers toxoplasmosis prophylaxis (indicated below CD4 100 with positive IgG). Prophylaxis can be stopped once CD4 sustains above 200 for at least 3 to 6 months on antiretroviral therapy. Mycobacterium avium complex prophylaxis with azithromycin is indicated below CD4 50.
key_points: PJP prophylaxis: cotrimoxazole when CD4 below 200 or below 14 percent.
Toxoplasma prophylaxis (cotrimoxazole) when CD4 below 100 plus IgG positive.
explanation.pearls: In sulfa allergy, alternatives include dapsone 100 mg daily after checking G6PD status, or atovaquone 1500 mg daily with food; pentamidine is reserved for cases when oral agents fail.

stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the emergency department from her aged care facility with 24 hours of progressive confusion, fever, and reduced oral intake. She has been catheterised long term for retention. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease. She lives in residential aged care and is normally fully ambulant. On arrival she is rousable to voice but disoriented. Her urinalysis shows leukocytes and nitrites, and the urine in the bag is cloudy. Blood cultures have been taken. Her venous blood gas has just come back from the analyser.

The ED team is preparing the immediate resuscitation plan.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse rate 116/min, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, respiratory rate 26/min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 38.6°C
data_table.Examination: Rousable to voice, disoriented; warm peripheries with capillary refill 3 seconds; chest clear; suprapubic tenderness
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.31, lactate 4.2 mmol/L; creatinine 145 micromol/L (baseline 110); urine leukocytes and nitrites; blood cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate hour-1 sepsis bundle intervention?

options.text: Give a 250 mL crystalloid fluid challenge and reassess before any further fluid resuscitation begins.
options.rationale: Septic shock with lactate at 4.2 mmol/L requires 30 mL/kg early fluid, not a 250 mL challenge approach. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Defer fluid resuscitation pending CT scan to identify the source of sepsis before resuscitating.
options.rationale: Resuscitation runs in parallel with source identification; imaging must not delay fluids in shock. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline 0.05 mcg/kg/min as the very first intervention before any fluid bolus is given.
options.rationale: Vasopressors are started for ongoing hypotension despite adequate fluid resuscitation, not as the first action. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Give an IV stat dose of hydrocortisone 200 mg as first-line therapy for the haemodynamic instability.
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is reserved for septic shock requiring ongoing noradrenaline at 0.25 mcg/kg/min or more, not first-line. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Give 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid IV; commence broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour; recheck lactate at 2 hours.
options.rationale: Surviving Sepsis hour-1 bundle for septic shock with lactate above 4 mmol/L includes 30 mL/kg fluid, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and lactate clearance reassessment. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle for septic shock comprises: measure lactate (and remeasure if at or above 2 mmol/L), obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, begin rapid fluid resuscitation with 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate at or above 4 mmol/L, and start vasopressors if hypotension persists during or after fluid resuscitation to maintain mean arterial pressure at or above 65 mmHg. Source control is added as soon as feasible.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluid 30 mL/kg, vasopressors if needed.
Lactate above 4 mmol/L mandates fluid resuscitation regardless of blood pressure.
Target MAP 65 mmHg; start noradrenaline first-line if hypotension persists after fluids.
explanation.pearls: If lactate has not fallen by at least 10 percent after the initial 30 mL/kg, reassess for source control failure, occult bleeding, or evolving cardiogenic shock from sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy.
